subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 8. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, point, sing and play alongside the child, using the short scripts and activities set out here. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the days of the week in order and know a week has 7 days.
Use the words today, tomorrow and yesterday correctly.
Talk about morning, afternoon and evening and what happens in each.
Sort a group of school days and weekend days.
Name the parts of a clock: the face, the numbers, and the two hands.
Tell the hour hand (short) from the minute hand (long).
Read simple o'clock times on a clock.
Read simple half past times on a clock.
Put daily events in order using before and after.

concepts.title: The Days of the Week
concepts.explanation: Start with the seven days and their order. Young children learn the sequence best through song and daily repetition, long before they can read the words. Say the days out loud every single day: ‘Today is Wednesday.’ Point to the day on a calendar or card as you say it. How to teach it: Lay out seven cards, one for each day, in a line from Monday to Sunday. Say them together while touching each card. Then muddle the cards and ask the child to put them back in order. Sing the days-of-the-week song (to a tune the…
concepts.example: Once the order is secure, introduce today, tomorrow and yesterday. These words are tricky because they change meaning every day, so practise them fresh each morning. If today is Monday, tomorrow will be Tuesday and…

concepts.title: Parts of the Day — Morning, Afternoon, Evening
concepts.explanation: Before clocks, help the child feel the shape of a day. There are three easy parts: morning (from waking until lunch), afternoon (from lunch until dinner) and evening (from dinner until bed). Children already live this rhythm — you are simply giving it names. How to teach it: Talk through the child's own day in order. ‘In the morning we wake for Fajr and eat breakfast. In the afternoon we have lunch and play. In the evening we pray Maghrib, eat dinner and get ready for bed.’ Ask the child to sort picture cards —…
concepts.example: This is also the perfect place to practise sequencing with the words before and after. Ask, ‘What do we do before we sleep?’ or ‘What comes after breakfast?’ Putting the events of a day in order is the foundation that…

concepts.title: Meeting the Clock — the Two Hands
concepts.explanation: Now bring out a clock. First, just look at it together. Point to the round face, the numbers 1 to 12 going around the edge, and the two hands that move. The single most important idea to fix here is which hand is which: the short hand is the hour hand, and the long hand is the minute hand. How to teach it: Use colour to make the hands easy to tell apart — for example, make the short hour hand red and the long minute hand blue, and use the same colours every single time. Point to each hand and say its job: ‘The…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Show me the short hand. What is its name?” Child: “The hour hand.” You: “Show me the long hand. What is its name?” Child: “The minute hand.” You: “Short hand tells the hour, long hand tells…

concepts.title: Reading O'clock Times
concepts.explanation: Begin with o'clock times only — they are the simplest and should be secure before half past is introduced. The rule is short and clear: when the long minute hand points straight up to 12, we say ‘o'clock’, and the short hour hand tells us which o'clock. How to teach it: Set the clock to 3 o'clock. Say together, ‘The long hand is on 12, the short hand is on 3 — it is 3 o'clock.’ Move the short hand around and read each new o'clock time. Tie times to the routine: ‘School starts at 9 o'clock,’ ‘Lunch is at 12…
concepts.example: Begin with o'clock times only — they are the simplest and should be secure before half past is introduced. The rule is short and clear: when the long minute hand points straight up to 12, we say ‘o'clock’, and the short…

concepts.title: Reading Half Past Times
concepts.explanation: Only move to half past once o'clock times are easy and confident. ‘Half past’ means we are halfway through the hour — thirty minutes past. Show ‘half’ with something concrete first: half of a piece of anjero, or half of a paper circle coloured in. Then explain that on a clock, when the long minute hand points down to 6, it is half past. How to teach it: Set the clock to half past 7. Point out that the long hand is on 6 (halfway round) and the short hand is between the 7 and the 8. Say, ‘It is half past 7.’ The…
concepts.example: Finally, mix o'clock and half past times so the child chooses which they are looking at. The single question to ask every time is: ‘Where is the long hand? On 12, it is o'clock. On 6, it is half past.’ This one check…

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: Begin with o'clock times only — they

questions.question: What does “Week” mean?
options: Now, the next day, the day before
Which event or day comes first / next
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Week means A group of 7 days.

questions.question: What does “Today / tomorrow / yesterday” mean?
options: Now, the next day, the day before
Which event or day comes first / next
The thing that shows us the time
questions.answer: Now, the next day, the day before
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Today / tomorrow / yesterday means Now, the next day, the day before.

selfAssessment: I can name the days of the week in order and know a week has 7 days.
I can use the words today, tomorrow and yesterday correctly.
I can talk about morning, afternoon and evening and what happens in each.
I can sort a group of school days and weekend days.
I can name the parts of a clock: the face, the numbers, and the two hands.
I can tell the hour hand (short) from the minute hand (long).
I can read simple o'clock times on a clock.
I can read simple half past times on a clock.
